Equity Research · Initiation of Coverage
Industrials — Aerospace & Defense
22 June 2026
Initiation of Coverage

StandardAero, Inc.

NYSE: SARO
InitiationAerospace & DefenseMid-cap · $9.1B

Executive Summary

StandardAero is the world's largest independent, pure-play provider of aerospace engine aftermarket (MRO) services — an installed-base-driven, ~80%-under-contract model with ~$6.25bn of LTM revenue compounding in the mid-teens. The market prices it on full-engine MRO's low-teens margin; the investable question is whether the higher-margin component-repair mix-shift and the free-cash-flow inflection re-rate it toward its engine-aftermarket peers. We initiate at Fair Value.

($mm unless noted) LTM 1Q26 NTM (consensus)
Revenue 6,254 6,425
Adjusted EBITDA 813 890
Net income 294 391
Enterprise value 11,184 —
Net debt 2,121 —

NTM = consensus / FY2026 guidance midpoint (Adj. EBITDA, FCF) and consensus EPS × shares (net income, calc.); EV and net debt are point-in-time. Subject figures are the step-4 model's LTM, identical to the Financial DD section.

Investment thesis

  • Share gainer in a steady market. Revenue compounds ~13.5%/yr with growth accelerating (9.9% → 15.8%), ~2–3x the ~5–6% engine-MRO market — i.e. share gains and over-indexing to growth platforms (LEAP, CFM56), not just riding the cycle.
  • Margin mix-shift in motion. CRS (28.8% margin, 11.5% of revenue) is growing ~20%/yr versus Engine Services (12.9%); group margin should inflect toward ~14% as the LEAP/CFM56 ramp matures.
  • FCF inflection + organic deleveraging. FCF swung from negative (FY22–24) to +$209m (FY25), guided to $270–300m for FY26; net leverage fell 5.6x → 2.6x, the last leg organic.
  • Priced fairly, not cheaply. 13.8x EV/LTM EBITDA is a ~24% discount to the engine-aftermarket core comps (MTU/FTAI/AAR median 18.2x); the headline 54% discount to the full-peer median (29.7x) is a margin/mix artefact (HEICO, Loar).

Key strengths

  • Authorization moat. Exclusive / semi-exclusive OEM service authorizations — sole independent in North America on several Rolls-Royce, Honeywell and Safran platforms; first independent LEAP-1A/1B Premier MRO in the Americas.
  • Recurring, installed-base revenue — ~80% under long-term agreements; sticky shop-visit demand.
  • End-market diversification (commercial / business aviation / military) smooths the cycle.
  • Accretive CRS roll-up (ATI, Unified Turbines) consolidating a fragmented, higher-margin niche.

Key risks

  • OEM dependence — the OEMs are authorizer, largest supplier and largest competitor; a key authorization non-renewal is a material, platform-specific risk (top-4 OEM suppliers = a majority of parts purchases).
  • Working-capital intensity gates cash. Contract-asset / unbilled WIP (~$1.25bn) drives a working-capital drag; LTM FCF of $139m sits below the FY25 $209m print on the seasonal build.
  • New-platform margin drag (LEAP, CFM56 DFW) near-term, and military / DoD budget & shutdown sensitivity.
  • Sponsor overhang — Carlyle retains a substantial post-IPO stake (secondary sell-down risk).

Competitive positioning

StandardAero is the largest independent pure-play engine MRO — distinct from the commercial-airline-centric large independents (Lufthansa Technik, MTU Maintenance) and the OEM service arms (GE, Pratt & Whitney, Rolls-Royce, Safran) that dominate new-platform aftermarket. Its edge is the breadth of OEM authorizations plus end-market diversification; its structural disadvantage is smaller commercial-engine scale than MTU/LHT and dependence on the very OEMs it competes with.

Entry verdict — Fair Value at $27.26. Cheaper than the full peer median but largely on margin/mix; broadly in line with the engine-aftermarket comps, and a 1.5% FCF yield below the ~4.5% risk-free rate removes the deep-value case until the FY26 cash inflection proves out. Timing — Wait: the technical read is deteriorating (price ~+1% vs EMA(100), momentum rolling over) — accumulate on a pullback toward the 52-week low (~$24) or on confirmation of the FCF inflection at the FY26 prints, rather than chasing here.

Company Overview↑ Contents

What StandardAero does, how it earns money, and the segment mix that drives the margin story.

Key takeaways

  • Largest independent pure-play engine MRO; ~$6.25bn LTM revenue, ~80% under long-term agreements.
  • Profit is bifurcated — CRS earns a ~28.8% margin on 11.5% of revenue vs Engine Services' ~12.9%.
  • The moat is intangible: exclusive/semi-exclusive OEM authorizations — durable but authorization-dependent.

Initiating-coverage Company Overview (IC pipeline step 2). Primary sources (co-equal): SARO_DATAROOM filings and the official company website / IR; reputable third-party web sources used where they add value (cited). Reported figures from filings/company site are stated without citation; (calc.) = derived, (est.) = estimate. LTM anchor: trailing twelve months through Q1 2026 (quarter ended 31-Mar-2026); all "LTM 1Q26" figures use this window. Fiscal year = calendar year.

Company Description↑ Contents

StandardAero is the world's largest independent, pure-play provider of aerospace engine aftermarket services — engine maintenance, repair and overhaul (MRO), engine component repair, on-wing/field service, asset management and engineering — for commercial, military and business-aviation operators. It sits between engine OEMs and aircraft operators in the aftermarket value chain, with more than 100 years of operating history and a portfolio of exclusive and semi-exclusive OEM service authorizations that is the core of the investment case.

The business performs scheduled and unscheduled shop maintenance on jet engines and their high-value components and returns them to service under quality and turnaround commitments. Revenue scales with the installed base of engines StandardAero is authorized to service, the frequency of shop visits on those platforms, and the parts and labour content of each visit — an installed-base-driven model rather than a project or capex-cycle one. Operations split into two reportable segments: Engine Services (full-engine MRO, the scale business) and Component Repair Services / CRS (specialised component repair, structurally higher-margin), with ~80% of revenue under long-term customer agreements.

LTM 1Q26 revenue was approximately $6,253.7m with Adjusted EBITDA of approximately $813.2m, a ~13.0% Adjusted EBITDA margin (calc.: LTM revenue = FY2025 $6,062.5m + Q1 2026 − Q1 2025 segment revenue; LTM Adjusted EBITDA = FY2025 $808.2m + Q1 2026 $203.2m − Q1 2025 $198.2m). On a reported full-year basis, FY2025 revenue was $6,062.5m (+15.8% YoY) and Adjusted EBITDA $808.2m, a 13.3% margin — mid-teens revenue growth at a low-teens margin, which screens above-average for industrials. The open question for the thesis is how quickly the dilutive ramp of new platforms (LEAP, CFM56 DFW) converts from margin drag to margin support. ("Adjusted EBITDA" is management-defined; segment profitability is disclosed as "Segment Adjusted EBITDA.")

Product Offering↑ Contents

StandardAero's offering is a service portfolio rather than a product catalogue: - Engine MRO — scheduled and unscheduled overhaul of fixed- and rotary-wing engines across commercial, business-aviation, military and helicopter platforms. - Component Repair Services (CRS) — repair of high-value engine components (including hot-section parts), serving commercial aerospace, military, land & marine and oil & gas end markets; the higher-margin, faster- growing line and the company's stated strategic priority, though at ~11% of revenue it does not yet set group economics. - On-wing & field service, asset management, engineering solutions — mobile support, used-serviceable- material / asset management and engineering. - Capability detail by platform is published at https://www.standardaero.com (Engine Services and Component Repair Services capability pages).

Key Reporting Segments↑ Contents

Profit quality is bifurcated, and that is the heart of the segment story. At LTM 1Q26 Engine Services is ~88.5% of revenue at a ~12.9% margin, while CRS is ~11.5% of revenue at a ~28.8% margin — CRS contributes roughly a quarter of segment profit on an eighth of revenue.

Engine Services — full-engine MRO and the scale segment. LTM 1Q26 revenue ~$5,532.8m, Segment Adjusted EBITDA ~$711.5m, ~12.9% margin (calc.). Growth is led by commercial aerospace and the ramp of growth platforms (LEAP-1A/1B, the CFM56 "DFW Center of Excellence", and a CF34 license expansion); those new platforms are margin-dilutive while they move down the learning curve, which is why segment margin has held flat (~13.2% FY2024–FY2025) despite strong volume.

Component Repair Services (CRS) — specialised component repair; smaller, structurally higher-margin and faster-growing. LTM 1Q26 revenue ~$721.0m, Segment Adjusted EBITDA ~$207.7m, ~28.8% margin (calc.). FY2025 revenue grew 19.6% with margin up 250bp to 28.6%, helped by the Aero Turbine (ATI) acquisition, pricing and mix.

Consolidated reference (for assembly): FY2023 revenue $4,563.3m / Adj EBITDA $561.1m (12.3%); FY2024 $5,237.2m / $690.5m (13.2%); FY2025 $6,062.5m / $808.2m (13.3%); LTM 1Q26 ~$6,253.7m / ~$813.2m (~13.0%, calc.).

Read-through: the margin thesis depends on CRS mix-shift (accretive) outrunning the new-platform ramp in Engine Services (dilutive). The evidence is mixed near term — CRS margin is climbing (28.6% FY2025, +250bp) but its small base caps the group-margin uplift while LEAP/CFM56 DFW weigh on the larger segment.

Geographies↑ Contents

Operations span 11 countries — the US, Canada, the UK, France, Ireland, Singapore, Australia, the Netherlands, Romania, South Africa, Brazil and Kenya — concentrated in North America. Revenue from outside the US and Canada was ~32% of total in FY2025 (30% in FY2024). Geographic concentration is moderate and rising abroad; it reads as a diversification lever rather than a present risk.

Geography FY2025 % FY2024 %
US & Canada 68% 70%
International (rest of world) 32% 30%

Revenue Model↑ Contents

Roughly 80% of FY2025 revenue was under long-term customer agreements, with most of the rest repeat shop-visit work tied to the installed base — so revenue is relatively sticky for an aerospace aftermarket business, closer to a recurring annuity than to pure transactional MRO. The stickiness is partly behavioural rather than contractual: tenors run 1 to 30 years, but many contracts carry no minimum-volume commitment and some are terminable without penalty on notice, and StandardAero acts both as a direct provider to operators and as an authorised subcontractor to OEMs. Pricing takes three forms — time-and-material, fixed price per service event, and a small portion of fixed price per engine hour/cycle. The result is above-average revenue quality, but one still geared to flight-hour utilisation, OEM subcontracting volumes and engine-platform mix — not recession-proof.

Customers / Sales & Marketing / Distribution↑ Contents

StandardAero serves ~5,000 customers, but concentration is real even as it improves: the top four OEM customers were ~36% of revenue in FY2025, down from 41% in FY2024 and 43% in FY2023. The structurally important feature is that OEMs are simultaneously authorizers, suppliers and customers (they subcontract MRO work to StandardAero) — a relationship that embeds the company in OEM workflows but concentrates bargaining power with a handful of OEMs and caps pricing leverage. Go-to-market is direct: in-house marketing, trade shows and a dedicated sales force, a very limited agent network, and a formal "Contract Tender Acceptance" gate for large program and new-platform bids. The named customers behind the top-four figure are not disclosed.

Demand Drivers↑ Contents

Demand is cyclical but cushioned — secular shop-visit growth on top of a cyclical air-traffic base. The lead drivers (growth rates as reported): - Commercial aerospace — the largest and fastest-growing end market; FY2025 commercial revenue +17.6% YoY, driven by air-traffic growth, high engine utilization and shop-visit demand outpacing capacity. - Business aviation — FY2025 +12.1% YoY; mid-size and super-mid-size platform demand. - Military & helicopter — FY2025 +9.4% YoY; ~17.8% of FY2025 revenue ($1,081.0m), tied to DoD and allied budgets and to outsourcing of depot-level maintenance (capped by the US "50% core" rule), and exposed to shutdown risk — a US government shutdown dented Q4 2025 military volumes. - New-platform ramps — LEAP, CFM56 DFW and CF34 expansions add incremental shop volume.

Near-term visibility is high (FY2026 guidance assumes low-double-digit to mid-teens commercial, high-single- digit military & helicopter and business aviation). The longer-term growth quality depends on new platforms outpacing the flat-to-declining installed base of mature platforms, a structural headwind the company flags.

Cost Drivers↑ Contents

The cost base is dominated by parts and materials (sourced largely from OEMs and designated distributors — see Suppliers) and skilled technician labour, with facility overhead behind them. Materials and direct labour are broadly variable with shop volume, so the model is volume-geared. The net effect is that margin direction is set more by mix (CRS and richer platforms), pricing and the new-platform learning curve than by input inflation, which is largely passed through under the contract structures — supportive of structural margin resilience, with near-term dilution from the LEAP/CFM56 DFW ramps. The precise fixed/variable split is not disclosed, so this read is partly inferred (see gaps file).

AI Considerations↑ Contents

The filings do not set out an explicit AI thesis; on the evidence, AI reads as a modest net opportunity rather than a disruption threat. On the opportunity side, predictive maintenance and engine-health analytics could forecast shop visits and pre-position parts, machine-vision inspection could raise CRS repair yield and throughput, and scheduling/forecasting tools could cut turnaround time and working capital — all of which map to StandardAero's throughput-and-yield economics. The constraint is that OEMs control much engine-sensor and operating data, so OEM-led prognostics could capture some diagnostic value; AI does not threaten the physical repair itself. Not thesis-changing either way on current evidence.

Research & Development↑ Contents

As a services business, StandardAero discloses no traditional R&D-as-%-of-sales line; reinvestment goes into capabilities, certifications, tooling and new-platform onboarding (LEAP, CFM56 DFW, CF34) rather than product R&D. Low product-R&D intensity is appropriate to the model, and an R&D/sales ratio would understate the true reinvestment rate — capability and certification spend is the better lens. A peer R&D comparison is not applicable for a pure-play MRO and is not disclosed.

Suppliers↑ Contents

Supplier dependence is one of the clearest structural vulnerabilities. StandardAero sources parts, components and raw materials from OEMs and material suppliers, and OEM authorizations frequently require purchasing from the OEM or its designated distributors. Concentration is high: the four largest parts suppliers, all OEMs, accounted for a substantial majority of total parts purchases in FY2025 and FY2024. Because aviation-authority and OEM certification requirements make qualifying alternative suppliers slow, recent OEM and parts supply delays have constrained engine throughput across end markets — putting throughput and margin partly at the mercy of OEM parts availability and pricing. Specific supplier names and shares are not disclosed.

Employees↑ Contents

StandardAero employs approximately 8,000 people across its 11 operating countries. The binding operational constraint on growth is skilled-technician availability more than labour cost — hence expanded school partnerships, scaled internships and training centres in Winnipeg, San Antonio, Dallas and Van Nuys plus a new UK apprenticeship. The workforce is predominantly non-unionized, with collective bargaining agreements in the UK, Ireland and Australia, a works council in France and a statutory bargaining unit in the Netherlands — together ~10% of employees — which limits collective-bargaining disruption risk; there have been no material work stoppages and no specific upcoming renegotiation is disclosed. (A "~12,000 employees / 22 sites" figure in the filing's front-matter graphics conflicts with the audited ~8,000 Human-Capital disclosure used here — see gaps file.)

Key IP or Facilities↑ Contents

The core asset is intangible: a portfolio of exclusive and semi-exclusive OEM service authorizations and licenses — the legal right, and the competitive barrier, to service specific platforms. StandardAero is the only independent provider in North America officially authorised for several platforms, including the Rolls-Royce RB211-535, AE 1107, AE 2100 and AE 3007; the Honeywell HTF7000; and the Safran Arriel, and was the first independent CFM International LEAP-1A / LEAP-1B Premier MRO in the Americas. This is a genuine moat, but an authorization-dependent one: durable while licenses renew (historically they have) and materially impaired if a key one were lost or not renewed, with OEM consent rights on change of control adding a further dependency. Facilities span 11 countries (engine shops, component-repair shops and training centres); a facility-by-facility list with square footage and owned/leased status sits in Item 2 and is not yet extracted (see gaps file).

Key Acquisitions and Divestitures↑ Contents

M&A is a core, recurring growth lever — StandardAero is a private-equity roll-up under The Carlyle Group (pre-IPO sponsor) — and recent deals are CRS-focused and margin-accretive, which is strategically coherent. From the two available public 10-Ks (FY2024, FY2025) and earnings releases, most-recent-first: - Unified Turbines (announced Q1 2026) — adds hot-section component-repair capability and strengthens CRS. Seller, price and multiple not disclosed in the dataroom. - Aero Turbine, Inc. ("ATI") — acquired 23-Aug-2024 — engine component repair; contributed $64.5m of incremental revenue in FY2025 and lifted CRS margins. Seller, price and multiple not disclosed in the extracted sources. - Pre-IPO acquisitions (FY2021–FY2022 and earlier) — the earlier roll-up history predates the public filings and sits in the S-1 / 424B prospectus, which the dataroom excludes by design.

The five-year, most-recent-first list is constrained by the October 2024 IPO: only FY2024–FY2025 deal disclosure is in-dataroom. The capital-allocation read is therefore incomplete — undisclosed prices and multiples (the priority bounded-web items) prevent a returns assessment, which is the key diligence step before crediting M&A with value creation.

Collateral Overview↑ Contents

Balance-sheet collateral as of 31-Mar-2026 (Q1 2026 10-Q; prior-period 31-Dec-2025 in brackets). The asset base is working-capital-intensive and asset-light on fixed assets: - Contract assets, net — $1,247.3m [$1,071.7m]: the largest working-capital asset — unbilled work-in-process on long-cycle engine overhauls where revenue is recognised over time ahead of billing; up ~$176m in the quarter. - Accounts receivable, net — $880.0m [$654.4m]: allowance $13.2m (~1.5% of gross); up ~$226m on higher volumes. - Inventories — $762.6m [$827.7m]: parts and WIP; down ~$65m, consistent with the stated lean-inventory posture. - Prepaid expenses and other current assets — $60.4m [$42.8m]. Total current assets $3,064.7m [$2,896.5m]. - Property, plant & equipment, net — $582.9m [$580.0m]: ~9% of LTM revenue, consistent with a labour- and parts-intensive services model rather than heavy manufacturing.

Watch item: collateral value is concentrated in receivables and unbilled work-in-process (~$2.1bn combined), not fixed assets, so cash conversion hinges on turning contract assets into billed receivables and cash — a working-capital drag during high-growth periods and the key item to track in the financials.

Management Overview↑ Contents

  • Russell Ford — Chairman & Chief Executive Officer. CEO since October 2013 and Chairman since April 2019 (~12 years leading the company); 30+ years in aerospace and industrials, with prior senior roles at Precision Castparts (President, Carlton Forge Works and Dickson Test Group), ClearEdge Power (President & CEO), Prestolite Electric (President & CEO), Holley Performance Products (COO), Lockheed Martin (SVP, Operations) and AlliedSignal. MBA, Duke (Fuqua); BS Mechanical Engineering, Georgia Tech. Led the October 2024 NYSE IPO (Bloomberg; Georgia Tech Woodruff School, accessed 2026-06-21).
  • Daniel Satterfield — Chief Financial Officer & Treasurer. CFO since January 2023; 30+ years in finance. Joined from Honeywell Aerospace, where he was CFO of the ~$12bn aerospace portfolio for ~4 years; earlier senior finance roles at Gates Corporation, Eaton, Cooper Industries and Siemens. BBA Accounting, University of Georgia; CPA (StandardAero — Dan Satterfield appointed CFO, accessed 2026-06-21).
  • Background / litigation / controversy check: no material litigation, regulatory action, fraud or controversy involving either executive was identified across the company website / IR, Bloomberg, and general web and SEC searches; only routine Section 16 insider transactions appear. No material issues identified on the sources checked (accessed 2026-06-21).

This is an experienced, aerospace-credentialed team — a long-tenured CEO who led the IPO and a CFO drawn from Honeywell Aerospace — with no material governance red flags identified. The combined Chair/CEO role and Carlyle's residual board influence are standard post-IPO governance watch-items rather than current concerns.

Other Risks / Comments↑ Contents

  • Customer / OEM concentration (top four ~36%) and the OEM's triple role as authorizer, supplier and customer; loss or non-renewal of a key OEM authorization is a material, platform-specific risk.
  • Supplier concentration (top four OEM suppliers = majority of parts purchases) and OEM-mandated sourcing.
  • Mature-platform installed-base decline, which must be offset by share gains and new platforms.
  • New-platform margin drag (LEAP, CFM56 DFW) near-term, and military / DoD budget and shutdown sensitivity.
  • Sponsor overhang: Carlyle retains a substantial post-IPO holding, a potential secondary-sell-down overhang.

The dominant risks cluster around OEM dependence (authorizations plus parts supply), customer concentration and new-platform execution; none appears acute today, but each is monitorable and could re-rate the quality score if it deteriorates.

Industry & Competitive Landscape↑ Contents

The engine-aftermarket structure and growth, and where StandardAero sits in it.

Key takeaways

  • A ~$42–47bn, mid-single-digit-growth, OEM-gated market; SARO grows mid-teens — i.e. taking share.
  • OEM service arms dominate new platforms; independents lead mature fleets and multi-platform flexibility.
  • Component repair is fragmented — the consolidation runway SARO's CRS roll-up exploits.

Initiating-coverage Industry Overview (IC pipeline step 3). Market-sizing and share data are third-party (cited with access date); company-specific facts are from SARO_DATAROOM filings (no citation). Market-size estimates vary widely by definition — ranges are shown rather than false-precise point figures. FY = calendar year.

Industry Definition & Boundaries↑ Contents

StandardAero competes in the aerospace engine aftermarket — the maintenance, repair and overhaul (MRO) of aircraft engines and their components over an engine's in-service life. This is the largest and most technically demanding slice of the broader aviation MRO market, which also spans airframe (heavy maintenance), components, line maintenance and modifications. The boundary that matters for SARO is the split between engine MRO (its core) and engine component repair (its higher-margin CRS segment), and the line between OEM-controlled aftermarket work on newer platforms and independent / authorised work — the structural fault line that defines competitive access.

Industry Characteristics↑ Contents

The engine aftermarket is secular-growth with a cyclical overlay, highly regulated, and OEM-gated rather than freely contestable: - Secular but cyclical. Demand is driven by flight hours and engine shop-visit cycles, which compound with fleet growth, but track the commercial air-traffic cycle and (for the military slice) defence budgets. Maintenance is largely non-discretionary for airworthiness, which cushions downturns, though operators can defer or extend intervals at the margin. - Regulated. Work requires aviation-authority approvals (FAA/EASA and equivalents) and, critically, OEM authorizations/licenses to service specific platforms — a regulatory and commercial barrier in one. - Concentrated at the top, fragmented at the bottom. Engine MRO is led by a handful of OEM service arms and large independents; engine component repair is highly fragmented (many small specialists), which is the consolidation opportunity SARO's CRS roll-up exploits.

Metric Estimate Source
Commercial aviation MRO (2025) ~$119bn, → ~$156bn by 2035 (~2.7% CAGR); fleet 29,000 → 38,300 (+2.8% CAGR) Oliver Wyman
Engine MRO market (2025) ~$42–47bn (largest MRO segment; ~36% of total MRO spend) Mordor; Fortune Business Insights
Engine MRO CAGR ~5–6% consensus (wide range across firms, ~5%–13%) Mordor (6.4%); Insight Partners (5.0%)

Read-through: this is a mid-single-digit-growth end market in which SARO is growing mid-teens (FY2025 revenue +15.8%) — i.e. the company is taking share and/or over-indexed to the faster-growing platforms (LEAP, CFM56), not simply riding the market. Sustaining that gap is the core growth debate.

Key Industry Trends & CAGRs↑ Contents

  • Next-generation engine ramp (LEAP, GTF). The installed base is shifting to new-technology narrowbody engines whose early-life aftermarket is tightly OEM-controlled; the contest for independents is winning authorizations early (SARO is the first independent LEAP-1A/1B Premier MRO in the Americas).
  • Capacity-constrained MRO. Shop slots, tooling, parts and skilled labour are scarce, so demand is outrunning supply — supportive of pricing and utilisation, and a tailwind SARO has cited directly.
  • Aging mature fleets. Operators are extending the life of mature platforms (e.g. CFM56) to bridge new-build delivery delays, lengthening the high-margin mature-engine shop-visit tail.
  • OEM aftermarket capture. OEMs increasingly pursue "power-by-the-hour" and razor/blade models to retain aftermarket value on new platforms — a structural pressure on independents that authorizations only partly offset.
  • Component-repair consolidation and USM/PMA. A fragmented component-repair market is consolidating, while used-serviceable-material and PMA parts grow as cost-saving alternatives to new OEM parts.

End Markets & Demand Drivers↑ Contents

- Commercial aerospace — the largest pool; mid-single-digit market CAGR on fleet growth and shop-visit cycles. SARO's commercial revenue grew +17.6% in FY2025, well ahead of the market, on platform ramps and share capture. - Business aviation — growing on bizjet fleet expansion and high utilisation; SARO +12.1% FY2025. - Military & helicopter — tied to DoD and allied budgets and to the outsourcing of depot-level maintenance to the civil base (capped in the US by the "50% core" statute); SARO +9.4% FY2025, ~17.8% of revenue, and exposed to budget and government-shutdown timing. Current geopolitical tension is a near-term tailwind.

Demand drivers across all three are the same primitives: flight hours, the size and age of the installed base, shop-visit intervals, and the pace of new-platform introduction — with OEM authorization access determining how much of that demand any given independent can actually serve.

Industry Structure↑ Contents

Three groups serve the market: OEM service divisions (GE Aerospace, CFM International, Pratt & Whitney, Rolls-Royce, Safran), large independents / airline-affiliated MROs (Lufthansa Technik, MTU Maintenance, ST Engineering, SR Technics, AFI KLM E&M, SIA Engineering, and StandardAero), and airline in-house shops. On newer platforms the OEMs hold the dominant position — GE Aerospace is estimated to account for ~30%+ of engine MRO supply over 2020–29 and CFM ~28% of commercial MRO supply — because design authority, parts control and early-life data favour them. Independents compete across the installed base under OEM authorizations, and lead on mature platforms, multi-platform flexibility and cost. Engine component repair, by contrast, is highly fragmented.

Value Chain & Where the Company Sits↑ Contents

The chain runs engine OEMs (design + new-build + parts) → MRO providers (overhaul, repair, component repair) → aircraft operators / lessors → (end users), with aviation authorities and OEM authorizations gating who can perform what. StandardAero sits at the MRO node, dependent upstream on OEMs for authorizations and parts and serving downstream operators and the OEMs themselves (which subcontract overflow work). It is unusually positioned as both a licensee of and a subcontractor to the same OEMs that are also its largest competitors — the defining tension of the independent-MRO model.

Competitors & Emerging Entrants↑ Contents

  • OEM service arms (GE Aerospace, CFM, Pratt & Whitney, Rolls-Royce, Safran) — the dominant force on newer platforms, with design authority, parts control and the deepest resources; the standing risk is that they in-source more work or tighten authorization terms.
  • Large independents — Lufthansa Technik (the largest overall MRO) and MTU Maintenance (the largest independent commercial MRO, ~€6bn maintenance revenue in 2025, +18%, ~1,500 engines) are the scaled independent leaders, with ST Engineering, SR Technics, AFI KLM E&M and SIA Engineering also competing.
  • Component-repair specialists — HEICO at scale plus many smaller shops; the fragmented field SARO is consolidating via CRS acquisitions (ATI, Unified Turbines).
  • Airline in-house divisions and US/foreign military depots round out the field.
  • Emerging pressure comes less from new entrants than from OEM aftermarket capture on new platforms and from low-cost USM/PMA alternatives.

StandardAero's differentiation is its pure-play engine focus, breadth of OEM authorizations (exclusive or semi-exclusive on several platforms), and diversification across commercial, business-aviation and military end markets — a profile distinct from the commercial-airline-centric large independents.

Bargaining Power↑ Contents

  • OEMs — high (the key external lever). They control authorizations, parts, IP and design, can revoke or decline to renew licenses, and can choose to in-source aftermarket work; they are simultaneously SARO's licensor, largest supplier and largest competitor.
  • Customers (airlines, operators, lessors) — moderate. Large operators have negotiating weight, but authorization scarcity, switching friction and capacity tightness favour incumbents.
  • Suppliers (OEMs / designated distributors) — high, for the same reasons as above (see company Suppliers).
  • Regulators — high (airworthiness approvals; defence contracting rules).
  • New entrants — low power / high barriers (authorizations, certification, reliability record, scale).

Supply Constraints / Bottlenecks↑ Contents

The binding constraints are shop capacity, OEM parts availability, tooling/certification, and skilled- technician labour — the same scarcities that constrain SARO directly. Parts lead-times and OEM supply delays have throttled industry throughput in recent years, and certification requirements make alternative-supplier qualification slow. These bottlenecks cut two ways: they cap volume growth but support pricing and utilisation for providers that hold capacity and authorizations.

Structural Advantages / Disadvantages↑ Contents

Advantaged: scale among independents and a pure-play focus; a broad book of OEM authorizations (including sole-independent positions in North America on several Rolls-Royce, Honeywell and Safran platforms, plus early LEAP access); end-market diversification (commercial + business aviation + military) that smooths the cycle; and a growing, higher-margin component-repair platform in a fragmenting niche.

Disadvantaged / exposed: structural dependence on the OEMs that are also its competitors (authorizations and parts); smaller scale in commercial engine MRO than Lufthansa Technik and MTU and than the OEM service arms; and exposure to OEM aftermarket capture on the very new platforms (LEAP) that drive its growth.

Market Share↑ Contents

Platform-level and overall engine-MRO share is not reliably disclosed for SARO or most competitors, and market definitions differ enough that a precise figure would be false precision. What is supportable: SARO describes itself as the world's largest independent pure-play engine aftermarket provider, a defensible but definition-specific claim (MTU Maintenance is the largest independent commercial MRO; Lufthansa Technik the largest MRO overall). With ~$6.25bn LTM revenue against a ~$42–47bn engine-MRO market that spans work SARO does not address, a clean share number is not derivable. The more useful read is directional: SARO's mid-teens organic growth versus a ~5–6% market implies ongoing share gains, concentrated on growth platforms (LEAP, CFM56) and in component repair — the trend to verify rather than a static share to pin down.

Open Industry Questions for Further Diligence↑ Contents

  • OEM authorization durability on next-gen platforms (LEAP, and any GTF position) — terms, exclusivity and renewal risk as OEMs push power-by-the-hour models.
  • Sustainability of share gains — how much of SARO's growth-vs-market gap is platform mix (ramp timing) versus durable share capture, and for how long.
  • Component-repair consolidation runway — depth of the fragmented CRS opportunity and acquisition multiples (ties to the company M&A diligence items).
  • Defence-budget and outsourcing trajectory — direction of DoD spend and the "50% core" outsourcing cap.
  • Engine-MRO market sizing — reconcile the wide third-party range to a defensible TAM for SARO's served segments (commercial + bizav + military engine MRO + component repair).

References (third-party / web)↑ Contents

  • Oliver Wyman — Global Fleet & MRO Market Forecast 2025–2035 (accessed 2026-06-21)
  • Mordor Intelligence — Aircraft Engine MRO Market (accessed 2026-06-21)
  • Fortune Business Insights — Aircraft Engine MRO Market (accessed 2026-06-21)
  • The Insight Partners — Aircraft Engine MRO Market (accessed 2026-06-21)
  • Statista — World engine MRO manufacturer share (accessed 2026-06-21)
  • MTU Aero Engines — business fields & 2025 MRO results (accessed 2026-06-21)

Financial Due Diligence↑ Contents

The revenue/margin/FCF trajectory and the deleveraging path, on a continuing-ops LTM basis.

Key takeaways

  • ~13.5% revenue CAGR at a ~13% Adjusted-EBITDA margin; CRS mix lifting group margin gradually.
  • FCF inflected to +$209m in FY2025 (guided $270–300m FY2026); net leverage 5.6x → 2.6x, now organic.
  • Contract-asset working-capital intensity is the cash gate — the key item a credit lens tracks.

Step 4 of equity initiating-coverage research. Continuing-operations basis (= total company; no discontinued operations). $ in millions unless noted. Companion to StandardAero_Financial_Model_2026-06-22.xlsx. FY ends December 31. LTM = trailing twelve months to 1Q26 (= FY2025 + 1Q26 − 1Q25).

Context. StandardAero is the largest pure-play independent aerospace engine MRO (maintenance, repair & overhaul) provider, operating two segments — Engine Services (full-engine MRO, the scale business) and Component Repair Services / CRS (higher-margin component repair). Carlyle acquired the business in 2019; it IPO'd on 2 October 2024 at $24.00/share ($1,202.8m net primary proceeds), the proceeds used to repay debt. FY2022–FY2025 are clean post-LBO years; the IPO is a capital-structure event, not an accounting-basis break.

Comparability notes. (1) The balance sheet and all leverage / working-capital metrics begin FY2023 — the balance sheet is not in SEC XBRL before 2023 (10-K carries two-year BS comparatives) and the first 10-Q as a public company was 2024-Q3. (2) FY2022 Adjusted EBITDA and the FY2022 segment split are not disclosed in the dataroom. (3) Segment profitability is reported as Segment Adjusted EBITDA; the company's headline non-GAAP metric is Adjusted EBITDA.

Section 3 — Financial Trend Assessment & Analysis↑ Contents

3A. Multi-Year Financial Trend Analysis (FY2022–FY2025)

Revenue compounding, and accelerating. Revenue grew from $4,150m (FY2022) to $6,063m (FY2025), a ~13.5% CAGR, with the growth rate accelerating each year (9.9% → 14.8% → 15.8%). The driver is the commercial-aerospace aftermarket up-cycle plus the deliberate ramp of next-generation platforms — most importantly the CFM LEAP program (60 engines inducted in 2025 vs 10 in 2024) and the CFM56 "DFW Center of Excellence" — layered on a recovering military/helicopter book and steady business-aviation demand. ~80% of revenue sits under long-term customer agreements, so the base is recurring rather than project-driven.

Margin expanding slowly — the central tension. GAAP gross margin widened from 13.1% to 14.8% and Adjusted EBITDA margin from 12.3% (FY2023) to 13.3% (FY2025). The expansion is modest by design: the high-margin CRS segment (28.6% FY2025 margin, +250bp YoY) is mix-accretive, but it is only ~12% of revenue, and its uplift is being absorbed by the dilutive ramp of new platforms in Engine Services (LEAP/CFM56 DFW carry start-up economics while they move down the learning curve). The thesis hinges on CRS mix-shift and platform maturation outrunning that drag — the evidence is favourable but gradual.

Earnings inflection is a deleveraging story, not just an operating one. Net income swung from $(21)m / $(35)m losses (FY2022–FY2023) to $11m (FY2024) and $277m (FY2025). EBITDA growth helped, but the larger lever was interest expense collapsing from $310m (FY2023) to $174m (FY2025) as the IPO proceeds retired debt — i.e., the profit inflection is substantially a function of the capital structure, which a credit lens makes explicit.

Free cash flow & the deleveraging path (the core of the credit view). Free cash flow was negative every year FY2022–FY2024 and turned positive only in FY2025 (+$209m). Net leverage fell from 5.6x (FY2023) to 2.4x (FY2025) — but the heavy lifting was IPO-equity-funded, not organic: FY2024 financing shows the $1.2bn equity raise and a net ~$1.0bn debt paydown; gross debt has been essentially flat since (~$2.2bn). The FY2025 step from 3.1x to 2.4x, by contrast, was organic (EBITDA growth + cash build, no divestitures). Coverage improved in lockstep — interest coverage 1.8x → 4.6x, fixed-charge cover 1.6x → 4.2x. The structural cash headwind is contract-asset intensity: unbilled work-in-process on long-term MRO contracts consumed $80–157m of cash a year and now stands above total inventory on the balance sheet — the single biggest reason FCF lags reported earnings.

3B. Most-Recent-Quarter Commentary — 1Q26 (transcript-driven)

Top line strong and broad. 1Q26 revenue rose 13.3% to $1,626.9m, with all three end markets up double digits — Business Aviation +19.6%, Commercial Aerospace +11.4%, Military & Helicopter +10.3%. Management called out a sharp late-quarter recovery in the military business as activity normalised after the U.S. government shutdown, and lifted full-year military-and-helicopter growth guidance from high-single-digit to low-double-digit as a result. Net income grew 27% to $79.9m.

Headline margin optically weak, underlying margin fine. Adjusted EBITDA rose only 2.5% to $203.2m and margin fell 130bp to 12.5% — a deceleration that looks alarming next to +13.3% revenue. Management attributed it to the timing of engine shipments plus largely transitory/one-time items (including a CFM buyout cost that landed in March, which was contemplated in full-year guidance); excluding those, in-quarter margin was above 14%. The segment detail supports the "timing, not structure" read: Engine Services revenue +14.1% but Segment Adj. EBITDA only +2.7% (the LEAP/CFM ramp dilution), while CRS Segment Adj. EBITDA grew 10.6% with margin +90bp to 29.2% — the high-margin component-repair engine still firing.

Cash & balance sheet — seasonal, as expected. FCF was a $134m use, reflecting "typical first-quarter seasonality and working-capital timing" tied to the LEAP/CFM56 DFW ramp; management reiterated a 2H-weighted cash cadence with working capital declining in 2H. Net leverage was 2.6x (vs 3.1x a year ago), inside the company's stated 2–3x target. Capital allocation turned active: SARO repurchased $60m of stock in the quarter (the "Other, net" financing outflow in the Summary) and announced the acquisition of Unified Turbines, adding hot-section component-repair capability to CRS. For a credit reader, the intra-year takeaway is that leverage drifts up mid-year on the working-capital build (LTM FCF has eased to $139m from the $209m FY2025 print) before the Q4 harvest pulls it back — covenant headroom should be assessed against the seasonal peak, not the year-end trough.

3C. Most-Recent-Full-Year Commentary — FY2025 (transcript-driven)

A record year and the first full year public. FY2025 revenue grew 15.8% to $6,063m and Adjusted EBITDA 17.0% to $808m (margin 13.3%, +10bp). Management framed 2025 around three operating themes: (1) the LEAP ramp — 60 engines inducted (vs 10 in 2024), with 2H LEAP revenue ~2.5x the 1H level and 475+ proprietary LEAP component repairs now developed; (2) CRS outperformance — revenue +19.6% and margin +250bp to 28.6%, helped by the Aero Turbine (ATI) acquisition and component-repair capture running above plan; and (3) a deliberate exit of $300–400m of low-margin revenue that will lift reported margins prospectively.

Cash and capital allocation — the credit highlights. FY2025 delivered the first positive FCF year (+$209m), of which >$300m was generated in 2H (the seasonal pattern), and management guided to continued FCF growth in 2026 and beyond. Net leverage ended the year at 2.4x, which management explicitly tied to "meaningful capital-allocation flexibility" — the framework being organic investment first, value-accretive M&A second, and return of capital (the buyback) third. Two quality markers matter for the credit: the FY2025 deleveraging was organic (EBITDA + cash, no divestiture proceeds), and EBITDA quality improved sharply — total add-backs to Adjusted EBITDA fell from 25.0% of EBITDA in FY2024 (dominated by non-recurring IPO, refinancing and debt-extinguishment costs) to 8.5% in FY2025 (mostly the ongoing LEAP/CFM transformation cost plus stock comp). In other words, the 2.4x is struck on cleaner EBITDA than the prior-year ratio. One covenant note: the lenders' "Consolidated EBITDA" definition runs only ~$5m above reported Adjusted EBITDA — a tight, non-aggressive add-back package — and the company was in compliance with its springing first-lien net-leverage covenant.

3D. Synthesis — what the numbers say for a credit investor

SARO has executed a textbook post-LBO de-risking: leverage from 5.6x to 2.4x, coverage from 1.8x to 4.6x, and a swing to positive FCF, all in two years. But the next leg of deleveraging is qualitatively different — the IPO equity is spent, debt is flat at ~$2.2bn, and further progress must come from organic free cash flow, which the contract-asset working-capital intensity of long-cycle MRO structurally gates and which is heavily Q4-weighted. The bull case is that platform maturation (LEAP/CFM56 turning profitable in 1H 2026 per management), the low-margin-revenue exit, and CRS mix lift both margin and cash conversion from here. The watch items are intra-year liquidity/covenant headroom at the seasonal working-capital peak, the pace at which the LEAP/CFM ramp stops diluting margin, and capital-allocation discipline (buybacks + M&A) now that the balance sheet has optionality.

Section 4 — Guidance↑ Contents

FY2026 guidance was raised with the 1Q26 print (7 May 2026), the second consecutive increase over the initial February outlook:

Metric (FY2026) Current guidance (1Q26) vs initial (Feb 2026) Implied YoY
Revenue $6.325bn – $6.45bn +$38m at midpoint ~+5–6% (≈+12% incl. low-margin exit normalisation)
Adjusted EBITDA $875m – $905m raised $5m at the low end ~+8% to +12%
Adjusted EPS $1.40 – $1.50 +$0.05 ~+22% at midpoint
Free cash flow $270m – $300m reiterated step-up from $209m (FY2025)

End-market growth guidance was also raised — military & helicopter from high-single-digit to low-double-digit (post-shutdown recovery) and business aviation toward low-double-digit.

Read-through. The guidance implies (i) Adjusted EBITDA margin moving to ~13.8–14.0% (mid-point ~$890m on ~$6.39bn), continuing the gradual expansion as the LEAP/CFM ramp items and the low-margin-revenue exit roll through; (ii) continued FCF growth to ~$285m (midpoint), an implied FCF/Adj. EBITDA conversion of ~32% — a meaningful improvement on FY2025's 26% and consistent with management's "working capital to decline in 2H" framing; and (iii) on ~$285m of FCF plus EBITDA growth and roughly flat debt, net leverage should trend toward the low end of the 2–3x target absent incremental M&A or buybacks — the Unified Turbines acquisition and the active repurchase programme are the offsets to watch. The +22% Adjusted-EPS growth is flattered by the full-year benefit of lower post-IPO interest; on an EBITDA basis the growth is high-single/low-double-digit, which is the cleaner read on operating momentum.

Sources: SARO_DATAROOM — SEC XBRL companyfacts; 10-K FY2024 & FY2025; 10-Q 2024-Q3 through 2026-Q1; 8-K earnings releases and Q2-2025–Q1-2026 earnings-call transcripts; investor presentations. All figures derived in the companion Excel model on a continuing-operations basis; every statement ties to reported and the balance sheet balances each period. Net leverage ties to company-reported 3.1x (FY2024) / 2.4x (FY2025).

Valuation & Technical Analysis↑ Contents

Where StandardAero trades versus peers, and whether now is a good entry.

Key takeaways

  • 13.8x EV/LTM EBITDA — ~24% below the engine-aftermarket core-comp median (18.2x); fair, not cheap.
  • 1.5% FCF yield sits below the ~4.5% risk-free rate — priced for FCF growth, not current cash.
  • Verdict Fair Value; technicals say Wait — accumulate on a pullback or on the FY26 FCF inflection.

Step 5 of the initiating-coverage pipeline. Market data as of 2026-06-22. Subject figures are the step-4 model's LTM numbers (LTM to 1Q26), identical to the financials section — AV/Yahoo supplement only the market data the model lacks (price, market cap, 52-week range, forward EPS). Peers are sourced from two feeds only — Alpha Vantage COMPANY_OVERVIEW first, then Yahoo Finance for gaps (net debt, FCF, forward EPS, MTU) — with every multiple computed, not lifted. Peer EBITDA is reported (AV/Yahoo); the subject is the model's Adjusted EBITDA.

1. Overall Valuation & Entry Assessment↑ Contents

Headline: SARO screens cheap on the full peer median, but that median is inflated by structurally higher-margin component/parts names — against its true engine-aftermarket comps SARO is closer to fair value, and a free-cash-flow yield well below the risk-free rate removes the deep-value case.

Premium / discount — read it against the right comps. On the full six-name set SARO trades at 13.8x EV/LTM EBITDA vs a peer median of 29.7x (a ~54% discount) and 15.8x NTM P/E vs 30.7x (~49% discount). That optical discount is misleading: the median is dragged up by the high-margin component/parts peers — HEICO (28% EBITDA margin, 35.4x), Loar (35.9%, 38.9x) and FTAI (36.0%, 30.4x). Against the engine-/aftermarket comps the peer set says to weight — MTU 13.2x, AAR 18.2x, FTAI 30.4x (median 18.2x) — SARO at 13.8x is roughly in line with MTU and a ~24% discount to the core-comp median, not deeply cheap. The same holds on NTM P/E: SARO's 15.8x is in line with MTU's 16.6x. SARO sits among the lower-margin names (full engine MRO at 13.0% vs component/parts peers at 28–36%), which is most of what separates its multiple from the premium cluster.

FCF yield vs the cost of capital — the binding constraint. SARO's 1.5% LTM FCF yield, though marginally above the peer median (1.1%), sits well below the ~4.52% US 10-year (source, 2026-06-22) — a negative real cash yield, i.e. the equity is priced for FCF growth, not current cash. This ties to the step-4 credit finding: SARO's free cash flow is structurally gated by contract-asset working-capital intensity and is heavily Q4-weighted, so LTM FCF of $139m understates run-rate. On management's FY26 FCF guide of $270–300m the forward yield rises to ~3% — better, but still inside the risk-free rate. The cash case is improving, not yet attractive.

Upside (valuation drivers): - FCF inflection re-rates the worst multiple. SARO's 65x P/FCF is a function of depressed LTM FCF; delivering the ~$285m FY26 guide (≈3% forward FCF yield) pulls P/FCF toward the peer range and is the biggest re-rating lever. - Margin convergence. As the LEAP/CFM56 ramp matures (management guides those platforms profitable in 1H26) and the deliberate low-margin-revenue exit rolls through, EBITDA margin moves toward ~14%, narrowing the gap to the engine peers. - Continued organic deleveraging. Net leverage trending from 2.6x toward the low-2s on organic FCF (not divestiture proceeds) shifts value from creditors to equity.

Downside (valuation drivers): - No margin of safety on cash. A 1.5% FCF yield against a 4.5% risk-free rate leaves the equity exposed if the FCF inflection slips on the contract-asset/working-capital drag. - The discount may be deserved. A low-margin, working-capital-intensive full-MRO model structurally earns a lower multiple than the component/parts peers — the gap is not obviously an opportunity to close. - Capital-allocation drift. Active buybacks ($60m in 1Q26) plus M&A (Unified Turbines) compete with deleveraging now that the balance sheet has optionality.

Entry verdict — Fair Value at $27.26, with an attractive skew contingent on the FY26 free-cash-flow inflection landing. The "cheap vs peers" headline is mostly margin/mix; on the engine-aftermarket comps SARO is fairly priced (in line with MTU), and the sub-risk-free FCF yield rules out a deep-value call until cash conversion proves out.

Timing — Wait. The technical read is "Wait — deteriorating (low confidence)": price is only +1.0% above its EMA(100), RSI 54, momentum rolling over. Even at fair value there is no technical urgency — prefer to accumulate on a pullback toward the low-$20s / 52-week-low support ($23.83) or on confirmation of the FCF inflection at the FY26 prints, rather than chasing here.

2. Valuation Comparables↑ Contents

Subject + the six approved peers. Per the peer set, MTU / FTAI / AAR are the truest engine-aftermarket reads; HEICO / Loar / VSE are higher-margin component/parts read-acrosses and set an upper bound. Mean/Median over the peers only (subject excluded).

Ticker EBITDA Margin % EV/LTM EBITDA Mkt Cap $mm (bubble size)
SARO (subject) 13.0% 13.8x 9,063
FTAI 36.0% 30.4x 27,908
HEI 28.0% 35.4x 46,248
AIR 10.9% 18.2x 5,340
VSEC 15.2% 29.0x 6,048

As of 2026-06-22. Multiples computed from the inputs (EV = mkt cap + net debt; FCF yield = FCF ÷ mkt cap), not lifted from a vendor. NTM P/E = price ÷ consensus forward EPS. Stats over peers only; Core-comp rows over the direct engine-aftermarket comps (FTAI, AIR, MTX.DE); n/m and n/a skipped. EBITDA basis — SARO: Adjusted (step-4 model); peers: reported (AV COMPANY_OVERVIEW → Yahoo). MTX.DE absolute columns converted at EUR/USD 1.1427 (ratio columns are FX-invariant).

Data caveats

  • Subject (the ticker company) = the step-4 model's LTM numbers (Revenue $6,254m, Adj. EBITDA $813m, net debt $2,121m, FCF $139m) — identical to the financials section so the memo ties out. AV/Yahoo supplied only the market data (price, mkt cap, 52W) and forward EPS; the model's net debt ($2,121m) was used, not Yahoo's lease-inclusive $2,369m.
  • Core comps (weight these): FTAI, AIR, MTX.DE. The full-peer Mean/Median is pulled up by the high-margin component/parts read-across names (HEICO, Loar) — lean on the Core-comp rows (median EV/EBITDA 18.2x, NTM P/E 23.6x).
  • Peers from two feeds only — AV COMPANY_OVERVIEW first, then Yahoo Finance for net debt, FCF, forward EPS and the non-US name. Peer EBITDA is reported (AV/Yahoo), vs the subject's Adjusted basis — a basis difference that flatters SARO's relative margin/multiple somewhat.
  • FTAI: negative GAAP FCF → P/FCF n/m (leasing capex; the company quotes ~$915m "adjusted FCF"). VSE: net cash lowers its EV. MTU: FCF n/a (not returned by the feed). All skipped in the relevant stats.

3. Technical Analysis↑ Contents

Generated by the technical-chart skill — see technical-chart-SARO-20260622.html for the interactive 5-indicator chart (price + EMA100 + Bollinger Bands, RSI, MACD, Volume).

Tactical read: Wait — deteriorating (low confidence). Last $27.26, RSI 54, price +1.0% vs its EMA(100). SARO is hovering just above its 100-day trend with neutral momentum that is beginning to roll over — neither a breakdown nor a confirmed uptrend. There is no technical edge to entering here; the constructive entries are a pullback toward the 52-week low / support around $23.83 or a decisive reclaim of the EMA(100) on improving momentum. For a stock the fundamentals peg at fair value, the technicals argue for patience over a starter position at $27.
